title: Mojibake / Encoding Fixer
description: Fix garbled Japanese text (mojibake) from encoding errors. Reverses UTF-8, Shift_JIS, and EUC-JP mismatches. Free tool.
inputPlaceholder: Paste your garbled / mojibake text here...
hint: Example: æ—¥æœ¬èªž (UTF-8 Japanese text read with the wrong encoding)
noResults: No readable form found. The text may not be garbled, or it may use an unsupported encoding.

howToUse.label: Paste your garbled text
howToUse.description: Copy the garbled text from your email, webpage, or file and paste it into the input box. Click 'Try example' to see a live demonstration.

howToUse.label: Review the repair candidates
howToUse.description: The tool automatically tries 4 encoding combinations. Candidates marked 'Japanese detected' are the most likely correct decoding.

howToUse.label: Copy the correct text
howToUse.description: Once you identify the correct result, click 'Copy' to copy it to your clipboard.

faq.question: What causes mojibake (garbled Japanese text)?
faq.answer: Mojibake occurs when text encoded in one character encoding is read or displayed using a different encoding. For example, UTF-8 Japanese text read as Windows-1252 (Latin-1) shows as symbols like 'æ—¥æœ¬èªž' instead of '日本語'. Common causes include opening a UTF-8 file in old Windows software, or receiving emails with incorrect charset headers.

faq.question: Can this tool fix all types of garbled text?
faq.answer: This tool reverses the 'wrong encoding misread' by re-encoding garbled characters back to their original bytes, then re-decoding with Japanese encodings. It works best for UTF-8 text misread as Latin-1/Windows-1252, which is the most common case. Doubly-garbled text (encoding errors applied multiple times) or binary data corruption may not be recoverable.

faq.question: Does it support Shift_JIS and EUC-JP?
faq.answer: Yes. If the original text was Shift_JIS or EUC-JP bytes mistakenly read as Latin-1, the tool will show a candidate with the correct Japanese text. Look for the result marked 'Japanese detected'.

faq.question: Why is encoding such a common problem between Windows and Mac?
faq.answer: Windows historically defaulted to Shift_JIS or Windows-1252, while macOS and Linux default to UTF-8. This mismatch causes mojibake when sharing files across platforms or opening old emails in modern software. Windows 11 is gradually migrating to UTF-8 as default, but legacy software compatibility issues remain.
